sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “In the Morning I Lay My Requests Before You”

heb.paragraph: "In the morning (bōqer), LORD, you hear my voice; in the morning I lay my requests before you and wait expectantly" (v.3). Morning prayer is intentional: David arranges his requests before God like a priest arranging the morning sacrifice on the altar. The word ʿārak (arrange/lay out) is the sacrificial term for arranging wood and offerings.

refs.note: "Evening, morning and noon I cry out" — David’s threefold prayer habit.
"Very early in the morning, Jesus got up and went to a solitary place to pray" — the pattern David established.

notes.note: "In the morning I lay my requests before you and wait expectantly." Morning prayer is not casual but structured. David arranges his prayers and then watches for God’s response.
"For you are not a God who is pleased with wickedness... the arrogant cannot stand in your presence." God’s holiness excludes evil. This is not merely preference but nature.
"But I, by your great love, can come into your house; in reverence I bow down toward your holy temple." Access to God’s presence is by grace ("your great love"), not by merit.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "I array my words" — the prayer is composed, ordered, presented as an offering. Not spontaneous outpouring but deliberate arrangement. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "by your great love" is the access key. Calvin: no one enters God’s presence by right. Love (ḥesed) is the only door. This is OT grace. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ʿārak (lay out/arrange) is the technical term for arranging sacrificial offerings (Lev 1:7–8). Morning prayer is a verbal sacrifice. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "lay my requests" uses the priestly verb for arranging the altar sacrifice. David’s prayer is liturgical even when personal. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: Psalm 5 is a morning prayer balancing Psalm 4’s evening prayer. Together they cover the full day cycle: trust at night (Ps 4:8), petition at dawn (Ps 5:3). VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "wait expectantly" — the verb ṣāpâ means to look out, to scan the horizon. After praying, David watches for God’s response like a watchman scanning for dawn. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David asks God to hear his words, consider his lament, listen to his cry (vv.1–2). He prays in the morning and waits (v.3). God takes no pleasure in wickedness (v.4); the arrogant cannot stand before God’s eyes (v.5). David will enter God’s house in reverence and awe (v.7).

heb.paragraph: "Lead me (nəḥēnî), LORD, in your righteousness" (v.8). The request for guidance through enemy territory. David does not ask for information but for direction: make your way straight before me. The path is God’s righteousness, not David’s navigation.

refs.note: "Their throats are open graves" — Paul quotes Psalm 5:9 in his indictment of human sinfulness.
"The LORD watches over the way of the righteous" — the same protective guidance.

notes.note: "Lead me in your righteousness because of my enemies — make your way straight before me." The request: not my way but YOUR way. Make it clear because the enemies make everything confusing.
"Let all who take refuge in you be glad... you surround them with your favour as with a shield." The psalm ends with joy and protection. The morning prayer concludes with confidence.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "open grave" and "slippery tongue" are body-part images: throat, tongue. The enemies’ bodies are instruments of death disguised as speech. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: "lead me in YOUR righteousness" — not mine. Calvin: the path is God’s; we walk in it. Our righteousness is derivative, not original.
Calvin: God’s favour as a shield — the shield is not a wall that blocks but a presence that surrounds. The protected person moves freely within divine favour.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the "open grave" image is visceral: the enemies’ speech swallows and buries victims alive. Paul’s quotation in Rom 3:13 universalises the indictment.
The NET: ṣinnâ (shield) here is the large body-shield, different from the small māgēn of 3:3. God’s favour is the full-body shield.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "their throat is an open grave" — speech that kills. The throat produces words that bury people. Paul applies this to all humanity (Rom 3:13). Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm moves from petition to praise. The trajectory is common: ask → trust → rejoice. The morning prayer ends not in anxiety but in song. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "surround them with favour as with a shield" echoes Psalm 3:3 (shield around me). God’s protection is comprehensive — surrounding, not merely frontal. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David asks for guidance (v.8), describes the enemies’ deceitful speech (vv.9–10: no truth in their mouths, throats like open graves), and calls for their judgement (v.10). Then the psalm brightens: "let all who take refuge in you be glad; let them ever sing for joy" (v.11). God spreads protection over them; those who love God’s name rejoice (v.11). The psalm closes with divine blessing: "you surround them with your favour as with a shield" (v.12).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBut I, by your great love, can come into your house; in reverence I bow down toward your holy temple.ESVBut I, through the abundance of your steadfast love, will enter your house. I will bow down toward your holy temple in the fear of you.NIVHear my cry for help, my King and my God, for to you I pray.ESVGive attention to the sound of my cry, my King and my God, for to you do I pray.

rec.text: ""In the morning I lay my requests before you and wait expectantly." Morning prayer is not casual but structured. David arranges his prayers and then watches for God’s response."

rows.label: Lead Me — 5:8–12
rows.text: "Make your way straight before me." Open graves vs joyful refuge. Favour as a shield.

hebtext.note: "In the morning (bōqer), LORD, you hear my voice; in the morning I lay my requests before you and wait expectantly" (v.3). Morning prayer is intentional: David arranges his requests before God like a priest arranging the morning sacrifice on the altar. The word ʿārak (arrange/lay out) is the sacrificial term for arranging wood and offerings.

"Lead me (nəḥēnî), LORD, in your righteousness" (v.8). The request for guidance through enemy territory. David does not ask for information but for direction: make your way straight before me. The path is God’s righteousness, not David’s navigation.

thread.text: "Evening, morning and noon I cry out" — David’s threefold prayer habit.
"Very early in the morning, Jesus got up and went to a solitary place to pray" — the pattern David established.
"Their throats are open graves" — Paul quotes Psalm 5:9 in his indictment of human sinfulness.
"The LORD watches over the way of the righteous" — the same protective guidance.

themes.note: Worship peaks: morning prayer as sacrifice. Holiness: God’s nature excludes evil. Faith: waiting expectantly after prayer.
